---
title: "Model Context Protocol (MCP)"
author: "Scott Bell"
description: "An open protocol that standardises how AI models connect to external tools, data sources, and services."
date: "2026-03-27"
categories:
  - definitions
  - agents
  - architecture
  - protocols
---

## Brief Summary

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open protocol, developed by Anthropic, that standardises how AI models connect to external tools, data sources, and services. It provides a universal interface so that any MCP-compatible client can use any MCP-compatible server, decoupling the model from its integrations.

## Discussion

Before MCP, every AI tool integration was bespoke. Want your agent to query a database? Write a custom tool function. Want it to control a browser? Different integration. Search the web? Another one. Each integration spoke its own language, handled its own auth, and defined its own schema. It worked, but it didn't scale.

MCP introduces a client-server architecture where the AI application (the client) communicates with external capabilities (servers) through a standardised protocol. An MCP server exposes a set of {{< defn "Tool Use" >}} capabilities — functions with typed parameters and descriptions — that any MCP client can discover, understand, and invoke.

The practical impact is composability. A Chrome DevTools MCP server gives an agent 29 browser automation tools. A Slack MCP server gives it messaging capabilities. A database MCP server gives it query access. The agent doesn't need to know the implementation details of any of these — it just sees tools with descriptions and schemas.

This matters for {{< defn "Harness Engineering" >}} because MCP servers become a standard way to extend an {{< defn "Agent Harness" >}}. Rather than building custom tool integrations, harness engineers can compose MCP servers to give agents the capabilities they need. The server handles the complexity; the harness just routes tool calls.

MCP also enables a cleaner security model. Each server runs in its own process, can enforce its own permissions, and the client can whitelist which tools are available in a given context. This is relevant to the {{< defn "Lethal Trifecta" >}} — MCP gives you a natural boundary for constraining what an agent can do.

The protocol supports resources (data the server exposes for reading), tools (functions the model can call), and prompts (templates the server provides). Most practical usage centres on tools.

## Annotated References

*TODO: Add annotated references.*

## Updates and Amendments

- **2026-03-27**: Initial definition published.
